**Gender transition** is the process of [affirming](/wiki/Gender_affirmation "Gender affirmation") and [expressing](/wiki/Gender_expression "Gender expression") [internal sense of gender](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity"), rather than the sex [assigned at birth](/wiki/Sex_assignment "Sex assignment"). It is a recommended course of treatment for persons experiencing [gender dysphoria](/wiki/Gender_dysphoria "Gender dysphoria"),[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) providing improved mental health outcomes in the majority of people.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

A *social transition* may include coming out as transgender,[[a]](#cite_note-a-7) using a new name and pronouns, and changing one's public gender expression.[[7]](#cite_note-Brown&Rounsley-8) This is usually the first step in a gender transition. People socially transition at almost any age,[[8]](#cite_note-:3-9)[[9]](#cite_note-:4-10) as a social transition does not involve medical procedures. It can, however, be a prerequisite to accessing transgender healthcare in many places.[[10]](#cite_note-:5-11)[[11]](#cite_note-:6-12)

In [transgender youth](/wiki/Transgender_youth "Transgender youth"), [puberty blockers](/wiki/Puberty_blockers "Puberty blockers") are sometimes offered at the onset of puberty to allow the exploration of their gender identity without the distress[[12]](#cite_note-:7-13)[[13]](#cite_note-:8-14) of [irreversible pubertal changes](/wiki/Puberty "Puberty"). Upon reaching the age of consent, they become eligible to pursue a medical transition if it is still desired.

A *medical transition* may include [hormone replacement therapy](/wiki/Cross-sex_hormone_therapy "Cross-sex hormone therapy") (HRT), [transgender voice therapy](/wiki/Transgender_voice_therapy "Transgender voice therapy"), and [gender-affirming surgery](/wiki/Gender-affirming_surgery "Gender-affirming surgery"). The ability to start a medical transition is typically offered after a diagnosis of [gender dysphoria](/wiki/Gender_dysphoria "Gender dysphoria"),[[14]](#cite_note-j624-15) a form of [medicalization](/wiki/Medicalization "Medicalization"). In recent years, there has been a push for an [informed consent model](/wiki/Informed_consent "Informed consent") of transgender healthcare which allows adults to access HRT without a formal diagnosis.[[15]](#cite_note-c152-16)

Transitioning is a process that can take anywhere from several months to several years.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

This page uses topic-specific vocabulary. Below are some definitions to make this page more accessible:

### Identity-related terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Identity-related terms")]

* **[Assigned gender at birth](/wiki/Assigned_gender_at_birth "Assigned gender at birth")** (**AGAB**) is a term that refers to the sex or gender assigned to people when they are born. Assigned a gender at birth is based on physical characteristics, and does not account for internal gender identity.
* **[Gender expression](/wiki/Gender_expression "Gender expression")** refers to the external presentation of gender identity. Typically, a person's gender expression is thought of in terms of [masculinity](/wiki/Masculinity "Masculinity") (a [tomboy](/wiki/Tomboy "Tomboy"), a [butch lesbian](/wiki/Butch_and_femme "Butch and femme"), a [drag king](/wiki/Drag_king "Drag king")) or [femininity](/wiki/Femininity "Femininity") (an [effeminate male](/wiki/Effeminacy "Effeminacy"), a '[femboy](/wiki/Femboy "Femboy")', a [drag queen](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen")), but an individual's gender expression may incorporate [both feminine and masculine traits](/wiki/Androgyny "Androgyny"), or neither. One may express their gender through clothing, behavior, hair styling, voice, etc.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) It is not necessarily related to one's gender identity.
* **[Gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity")** is the personal sense of their own [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"). It may correlate with the gender assigned at birth, or differ from it. In most individuals, gender identity is congruent with their assigned gender. Those who do not identify with their birth gender may identify as *transgender*.
* **[Gender dysphoria](/wiki/Gender_dysphoria "Gender dysphoria")** is the distress a person experiences from a mismatch between their [internal gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity") and their [gender assigned at birth](/wiki/Sex_assignment "Sex assignment").[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-MorrowMessinger-20) People who suffer from gender dysphoria may *transition* in order to alleviate this distress.
* **[Cisgender](/wiki/Cisgender "Cisgender")** is an adjective that refers to people who identify the gender assigned to them at birth. Cisgender people may have any [sexual orientation](/wiki/Sexual_orientation "Sexual orientation") or [gender expression](/wiki/Gender_expression "Gender expression").
* **[Transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender")** is an adjective that refers to people who identify with a gender that differs from the one assigned to them at birth. Transgender people may have any sexual orientation or gender expression.
* **[Non-binary](/wiki/Non-binary_gender "Non-binary gender")** is a gender identity described as neither entirely male nor female (not one of the two binary sexes). Non-binary people may suffer from gender dysphoria, and may consider themselves transgender. Non-binary people may have any sexual orientation or gender expression.
* **Transitioning** refers to the process of [affirming](/wiki/Gender_affirmation "Gender affirmation") and [expressing](/wiki/Gender_expression "Gender expression") one's [internal sense of gender](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity"), as opposed to the gender [assigned to them at birth](/wiki/Sex_assignment "Sex assignment"). There are two major facets of gender transitioning: a social transition, and a medical transition; almost all transgender people will socially transition, and many will undergo some degree of medical transition.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [**Detransitioning**](/wiki/Detransition "Detransition") (sometimes also known as **re-transitioning**) is the cessation of transgender identity and/or a transition to a different gender, often to the natal gender.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### Medical-related terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Medical-related terms")]

It is important to note that most transgender individuals will receive few, if any, surgeries throughout their lifetimes and some may never receive HRT. Barriers to accessing medical transitioning can include: a lack of financing, a lack of desire, or a lack of accessibility. There is no one way to transition, and while a medical transition or surgery can absolutely be medically necessary for some individuals' personal wellbeing, no two transitions are the same.

* [**Puberty blockers / hormone blockers**](/wiki/Puberty_blockers "Puberty blockers") are medications used to block natal sex hormones. For transgender youth, hormone blockers may be offered at the onset of puberty to allow the exploration of their gender identity without the distress[[12]](#cite_note-:7-13)[[13]](#cite_note-:8-14) of [irreversible pubertal changes](/wiki/Puberty "Puberty"). Puberty blockers are considered reversible[[21]](#cite_note-:2-22) and their use is deemed safe and effective[[22]](#cite_note-23) as treatment for gender dysphoria in gender-diverse children[[23]](#cite_note-24) and [precocious puberty](/wiki/Precocious_puberty "Precocious puberty") in [cisgender](/wiki/Cisgender "Cisgender") children;[[24]](#cite_note-25) their use may be discontinued at any time if natal puberty-blocking is no longer desired.[[21]](#cite_note-:2-22) For transgender adults, hormone blockers may be offered in a course with cross-sex hormone replacement therapy in order to treat gender dysphoria.
* [**Hormone replacement therapy (HRT)**](/wiki/Transgender_hormone_therapy "Transgender hormone therapy") is a medical treatment that replaces the primary sex hormones in the body, in order to develop the secondary sex characteristics of the opposite sex. Masculinizing HRT uses a male sex hormone [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone "Testosterone"), while feminizing HRT uses a female sex hormone [estrogen](/wiki/Estrogen "Estrogen").
* [**Voice therapy**](/wiki/Transgender_voice_therapy "Transgender voice therapy") (or '[**voice training**](/wiki/Transgender_voice_therapy "Transgender voice therapy")') is *non-surgical* gender-affirming treatment for the masculinization or feminization of the voice. *Transfeminine people* will not experience any impact on voice pitch from feminizing hormone therapy (unless their natal puberty was sufficiently blocked), so voice training is very often undergone in order to learn how to speak in a higher and more feminine register. *Transmasculine people*, on the other hand, will experience a marked lowering in pitch from masculinizing HRT (testosterone) to an often cisgender male level, so vocal masculinization training is uncommon outside of those who have not undergone masculinizing HRT. Irrespectively, vocal masculinization training can help one learn to speak in a lower and more masculine register.
* [**Voice surgery**](/wiki/Transgender_voice_therapy#Vocal_surgeries "Transgender voice therapy") refers to a *surgical* treatment that allows for the masculinization or feminization of one's vocal pitch. *Transfeminine people* can undergo voice surgery to surgically increase their pitch range. Transmasculine people very uncommonly undergo voice surgery, as masculinizing HRT (testosterone) often lowers voices to a cisgender male pitch. Irrespectively, vocal masculinization surgery can be underwent to surgically decrease their pitch range.
* **Facial surgery** is gender-affirming surgery underwent on the face, usually [facial masculinization](/wiki/Facial_masculinization_surgery "Facial masculinization surgery") or [facial feminization](/wiki/Facial_feminization_surgery "Facial feminization surgery").
* **[Top surgery](/wiki/Gender-affirming_surgery#Other_surgeries "Gender-affirming surgery")** is gender affirming surgeries of the breasts. In transmasculine and/or nonbinary individuals, this may be a [double mastectomy](/wiki/Mastectomy "Mastectomy") (removal of breast tissue), and/or a '['chest reconstruction](/wiki/Male_chest_reconstruction "Male chest reconstruction")'. In transfeminine and/or nonbinary individuals, this may be a [breast augmentation](/wiki/Breast_augmentation "Breast augmentation").
* **[Bottom surgery](/wiki/Bottom_surgery "Bottom surgery")** is gender affirming surgery performed on the genitalia. In transmasculine and/or nonbinary individuals, this may be a [hysterectomy](/wiki/Hysterectomy "Hysterectomy") (removal of the [uterus](/wiki/Uterus "Uterus")), and/or a [oophorectomy](/wiki/Oophorectomy "Oophorectomy") (removal of the [ovaries](/wiki/Ovaries "Ovaries")). A penis can be constructed through [metoidioplasty](/wiki/Metoidioplasty "Metoidioplasty") or [phalloplasty](/wiki/Phalloplasty "Phalloplasty"), and a [scrotum](/wiki/Scrotum "Scrotum") through [scrotoplasty](/wiki/Scrotoplasty "Scrotoplasty"). In transfeminine and/or nonbinary individuals, this may be a [penectomy](/wiki/Penectomy "Penectomy") (removal of the penis), [orchiectomy](/wiki/Orchiectomy "Orchiectomy") (removal of the testicles), [vaginoplasty](/wiki/Vaginoplasty "Vaginoplasty") (construction of a vagina), and/or a [vulvoplasty](/wiki/Vulvoplasty "Vulvoplasty") (construction of a vulva).

### Social-related terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Social-related terms")]

* **Going 'full-time'** refers to the act of living everyday life as one's identified gender.[[25]](#cite_note-social_transition-26) People who go full-time may or may not pass, and may or may not keep their transgender identity a secret.
* **[Passing](/wiki/Passing_(gender) "Passing (gender)")** refers to the perception and recognition of trans people as their desired gender identity by outsiders who may not know they are transgender. For many trans people, passing is a very important aspect of their transition often seen as an 'end goal'. Passing can greatly alleviate gender dysphoria. Failure to pass can cause serious repercussions for trans people's psychological well-being and safety, including but not limited to: [poor mental health](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), [discrimination](/wiki/Transgender_discrimination "Transgender discrimination"), increased barriers to medical care[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], harassment, fetishization, ostracization, increased risk of homelessness, and [targeted violence](/wiki/Trans_bashing "Trans bashing") due to increased visibility of one's transgender status.[[26]](#cite_note-27)
* **Going 'stealth'** refers to the act of living as one's identified gender without revealing oneself to be transgender.[[27]](#cite_note-28) In some countries, being stealth may be a safety necessity, due to health, safety, and wellbeing risks of being openly transgender.

## Various aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Various aspects")]

Transitioning is a complicated process that involves any or all of the gendered aspects of a person's life, which include aesthetics, social roles, legal status, and biological aspects of the body. People may choose elements based on their own gender identity, body image, personality, finances, and sometimes the attitudes of others. A degree of experimentation is used to know what changes best fit them. Transitioning also varies greatly between cultures and subcultures according to differences in the societies' views of gender.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### Social aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Social aspects")]

"Social transitioning" redirects here. For social changes on a societal level, see [Social change](/wiki/Social_change "Social change").

The social process of transitioning begins with '[coming out](/wiki/Coming_out "Coming out")', where others are told that one does not identify with their [birth sex](/wiki/Sex_assignment "Sex assignment"). The newly out trans person may adopt a new name, ask to be referred to with a new set of pronouns, and change their presentation to better reflect their identity.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-Bigner-31) Socially transitioning does not involve medical intervention or gender affirming surgery, but it may be a prerequisite to access transgender healthcare in some regions.[[10]](#cite_note-:5-11)[[11]](#cite_note-:6-12)

People may socially transition at any age, with documented cases of children as [young as 5](/wiki/Transgender_youth "Transgender youth"),[[8]](#cite_note-:3-9)[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) or adults as old as 75.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-10) While many of those who socially transition will pursue a medical transition, not everyone can access gender affirming care, and not all may wish to pursue it.

### Psychological aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Psychological aspects")]

A person's ideas about gender in general may change as part of their transition, which may affect their [religious](/wiki/Transgenderism_and_religion "Transgenderism and religion"), philosophical and/or political beliefs. In addition, personal relationships can take on different dynamics after coming out. For instance, what was originally a lesbian couple may become a heterosexual one as a partner comes out as a trans man - or parents of a boy may become parents of a girl after their child comes out as a trans woman.

### Legal aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Legal aspects")]

Main articles: [Transgender rights](/wiki/Transgender_rights "Transgender rights") and [Legal recognition of non-binary gender](/wiki/Legal_recognition_of_non-binary_gender "Legal recognition of non-binary gender")

Transgender people in many parts of the world can legally [change their name](/wiki/Name_change "Name change") to something consistent with their gender identity.[[30]](#cite_note-Bigner-31) Some regions also allow one's legal sex marker changed on documents such as [driver licenses](/wiki/Driver_license "Driver license"), [birth certificates](/wiki/Birth_certificate "Birth certificate"), and [passports](/wiki/Passport "Passport"). The exact requirements vary from jurisdiction to jurisdiction; some require [sex reassignment surgery](/wiki/Sex_reassignment_surgery "Sex reassignment surgery"), while many do not. In addition, some states that require sex reassignment surgery will only accept 'bottom surgery', or a genital reconstruction surgery, as a valid form of sex reassignment surgery, while other states allow other forms of gender confirmation surgery to qualify individuals for changing information on their birth certificates.[[33]](#cite_note-:1-34) In some U.S. states, it is also possible for transgender individuals to legally change their gender on their drivers license without having had any form of qualifying gender confirmation surgery.[[33]](#cite_note-:1-34) Also, some U.S. states are beginning to add the option of legally changing one's gender marker to X on legal documents, an option used by some [non-binary](/wiki/Non-binary_gender "Non-binary gender") people.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

### Physical aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Physical aspects")]

Main article: [Gender-affirming care](/wiki/Gender-affirming_care "Gender-affirming care")

Physical aspects of gender transition can go along with social aspects; as well as wearing gender affirming clothing, transgender people often hide features from their natal puberty, with many transgender men [binding their breasts](/wiki/Breast_binding "Breast binding") and transgender women shaving. Other physical aspects of transitioning require medical intervention, such as [transgender hormone therapy](/wiki/Transgender_hormone_therapy "Transgender hormone therapy") or surgeries.

### Grieving gender identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gender_transition&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Grieving gender identity")]

Over the course of a gender transition, people who are close to the transitioning individual may experience a sense of loss and work through a [grieving](/wiki/Grief "Grief") process.[[35]](#cite_note-:0-36) This type of loss is an [ambiguous loss](/wiki/Ambiguous_loss "Ambiguous loss"), characterized by feelings of grief where the item of loss is obscure. Family members may grieve for the gendered expectations that their loved one will no longer follow, whereas the transgender person themself may feel rejected by their relatives' need to grieve.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Feelings that arise are described as a way of seeing the person who is transitioning as the same, but different, or both present and absent.[[35]](#cite_note-:0-36)
